NG=F.name: Natural Gas
NG=F.explanation: Natural Gas is NEUTRAL as the CLF ensemble (49% bullish probability) sits right at the fence with very low conviction. Seasonally, late-June demand from air conditioning is offset by high storage levels and mild weather forecasts. The model awaits a clear EIA storage catalyst before committing directional. Risk: any unexpected heat wave or LNG export surge could quickly flip this to BULLISH.

GC=F.name: Gold Futures
GC=F.explanation: Gold is BULLISH with strong conviction (CLF 80% bull probability). Gold has corrected ~10% from its May highs near $4,686, presenting a reloading opportunity. Dollar strength (DXY 100.85) is a headwind, but neutral rate regime and persistent central bank buying underpin support. The model's ML ensemble sees structural demand intact. Key risk: a hawkish Fed surprise could push DXY above 102 and pressure gold.

SI=F.name: Silver Futures
SI=F.explanation: Silver is BULLISH (CLF 71% bull probability) riding gold's coattails with added industrial demand tailwinds. Silver has corrected more than gold from its highs, creating potential for outperformance on any precious metals recovery. The gold/silver ratio has widened, historically a mean-reversion opportunity favoring silver longs. Risk: a global manufacturing slowdown could undercut industrial demand support.

CL=F.name: WTI Crude Oil
CL=F.explanation: WTI Crude is BULLISH with strong conviction (CLF 80% bull probability). Oil has fallen sharply from $105 in May to $76.60, and the model sees this as oversold. OPEC+ production discipline is holding, and summer driving season demand should provide seasonal support. Dollar strength creates near-term friction but the fundamental supply-demand picture favors a bounce. Risk: recession fears or demand destruction data could accelerate the decline.

HG=F.name: Copper Futures
HG=F.explanation: Copper is BULLISH (CLF 71% probability) supported by Chinese infrastructure stimulus expectations and constrained mine supply from Peru and Chile. Copper at $6.37/lb has held technical support and the ML ensemble sees momentum building. Dollar strength (DXY 100.85) is the main headwind. Risk: China economic data disappointing expectations would be the key downside trigger.

HO=F.name: Heating Oil
HO=F.explanation: Heating Oil is BULLISH with highest conviction of any commodity (CLF 84%). Despite being out of peak season, refinery maintenance shutdowns are tightening distillate supply, and jet fuel demand remains strong. The model sees this setup as favorable for a near-term price recovery. Risk: a warmer-than-expected summer forecast revising demand expectations lower.

PA=F.name: Palladium
PA=F.explanation: Palladium is NEUTRAL with the CLF at 62% — above threshold but conviction is moderate. Palladium faces secular headwinds from EV adoption reducing catalytic converter demand, but near-term supply from Russia remains uncertain. The model sits on the fence without a strong directional edge. Risk: any news on Russian supply restrictions would be the catalyst to break this neutrality.

EURUSD=X.name: Euro / US Dollar
EURUSD=X.explanation: EUR/USD is BULLISH (CLF 66%, 3yr backtest OOS accuracy 73.4% — the model's best-performing pair). The ECB is signaling a pause in its cutting cycle while the Fed faces growth concerns that could eventually require easing. EUR at 1.1459 has room to recover toward 1.16-1.17 as dollar strength from DXY 100.85 appears stretched. This is the model's highest-confidence FX signal. Risk: US payrolls surprise to the upside, reigniting dollar demand.

GBPUSD=X.name: GBP / US Dollar
GBPUSD=X.explanation: GBP/USD is NEUTRAL with the CLF at 69% — strong signal but just below conviction threshold. UK economic resilience versus US growth uncertainty creates a tug-of-war. The model (3yr OOS 67.3%) sees the risk as balanced near 1.3202. A clear break of 1.33 could trigger a BULLISH signal. Risk: BOE dovish surprise or UK inflation coming in below expectations would weigh on sterling.

USDJPY=X.name: USD / JPY
USDJPY=X.explanation: USD/JPY is BULLISH (CLF 82% probability) reflecting continued yen weakness as the BOJ maintains ultra-loose policy while US rates hold elevated. At 161.29, USD/JPY is testing multi-decade highs — intervention risk from the BOJ is elevated but hasn't materialized. The ML ensemble strongly favors further yen weakness. Key risk: BOJ surprise rate hike or verbal intervention commitment could trigger sharp yen reversal.

AUDUSD=X.name: AUD / USD
AUDUSD=X.explanation: AUD/USD is NEUTRAL (CLF 57%, model 3yr OOS 64.1%). The Aussie is caught between risk-on tailwinds from Chinese stimulus hopes and dollar headwinds from DXY strength. At 0.7013, the pair sits near its trading range midpoint. The model's edge comes primarily from identifying trending regimes, and the current choppy environment reduces signal confidence. Risk: China economic acceleration would be the key BULLISH catalyst.

USDCHF=X.name: USD / CHF
USDCHF=X.explanation: USD/CHF is NEUTRAL — no CLF model is deployed for this pair currently. The Swiss franc remains a safe-haven currency with the SNB maintaining negative real rates. At 0.8049, the pair is range-bound. Calibration accuracy of 100% in the 30d window shows price band accuracy is excellent even without a directional model. This pair serves as a macro barometer.

DX-Y.NYB.name: US Dollar Index
DX-Y.NYB.explanation: DXY is NEUTRAL despite the CLF suggesting mild bearish pressure (35% bull probability). At 100.85, dollar strength persists but the model doesn't see enough edge to issue a directional call given the balanced macro backdrop. Neutral rates regime and mixed growth signals create uncertainty. A conviction BEARISH call would require US growth data to clearly weaken. Risk: hawkish Fed rhetoric could reignite dollar buying.

^TNX.name: US 10-Year Yield
^TNX.explanation: US 10Y yield at 4.451% is NEUTRAL with conflicting signals — the CLF (44% bull) suggests mild bearish pressure on yields while Polymarket shows 83% bullish probability (higher yields), creating model-market divergence. The Fed's data-dependent stance keeps yields range-bound between 4.2-4.6%. Neutral rate regime classification confirms this equilibrium. Risk: inflation surprise or fiscal deficit widening could push yields to new highs.

^IRX.name: US 3-Month T-Bill
^IRX.explanation: US 3-Month yield is BEARISH (CLF 24% bull probability, model OOS 59.1%) — the strongest directional call on rates. The model's ML ensemble sees mounting pressure for rate cuts reflected in short-end yields. At 3.658%, the 3M T-bill is pricing in Fed cuts over the coming months. This is the model's most confident rate call. Risk: stronger US economic data reducing Fed cut expectations would invalidate this.

^GSPC.name: S&P 500
^GSPC.explanation: S&P 500 is BULLISH (CLF 63%) at 7,500 — near all-time highs. The model sees momentum continuation despite stretched valuations. Low VIX (16.85) signals market complacency but also low hedging costs. Neutral rate regime and dollar strength haven't derailed equities. The ML ensemble's 3yr OOS accuracy of 51.8% on this index reflects its inherent difficulty to forecast short-term. Risk: earnings disappointments or a sudden VIX spike could trigger rapid de-risking.

^VIX.name: VIX (Fear Index)
^VIX.explanation: VIX is NEUTRAL at 16.85 — well below the 20 panic threshold. The model (OOS 57.9%) doesn't see a volatility catalyst in the near term. Low VIX is consistent with the risk-on market structure and strong equity prices. Consecutive band hits of 40 shows the model has been excellently calibrated on VIX. Key risk: any geopolitical shock, credit event, or surprise economic data could spike VIX sharply.

HYG.name: High Yield Bonds
HYG.explanation: High Yield Bonds (HYG) are NEUTRAL with the CLF at 54% — just barely above 50/50. At $80.01, HYG has recovered from recent lows and spread compression has slowed. Low VIX and strong equity markets provide support, but elevated Treasury yields cap the upside for credit. The model awaits a clearer directional signal. Risk: any credit market stress or leveraged loan defaults could cause rapid spread widening.
